titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Ronald Paulson Papers
titlestmt.author: by Sandra Markham and LWL staff

did.abstract: The Ronald Paulson Papers consist of his professional and personal papers, correspondence, military service records, writings, and research materials. Paulson was a leading scholar of British culture in the long eighteenth century, with a specialization in the artists, satirists, and social critics William Hogarth and Thomas Rowlandson, the authors Henry Fielding and Jonathan Swift, and the topics of satire and humor in general.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Ronald Paulson (Yale BA 1952, PhD 1958), 2018-2022, 2024

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into five series: I. Correspondence, 1952-2017. II. Personal Papers, 1938-2014. III. Writings, 1946-2018. IV. Research Files, 1957-2012. V. Research Photographs.

bioghist.head: Ronald Paulson (1930-2024)
bioghist.p: The American author, scholar, and professor Ronald Howard Paulson was born on May 27, 1930, in Bottineau, North Dakota, the only child of Howard C. Paulson (1900-1957) and Ethel F. Paulson (1901-1987). He was raised in Indiana where his father held administrative and leadership positions in public schools and with the Boy Scouts of America. Paulson graduated from Benjamin Bosse High School in Evansville, Indiana, in 1948, where he was editor and staff writer for The School Spirit newspaper. He entered Yale University that fall and earned a BA degree in English in 1952. As Scholar of the House in his senior year, he studied creative writing with Robert Penn Warren in lieu of attending any classes; he later destroyed the two novels he wrote that year after Martin Price criticized his talent for fiction. Before returning to Yale in 1954 for graduate school, Paulson served in the United States Army and was stationed in Japan. He was awarded a PhD in English from Yale in 1958, and his first book, Theme and Structure in Swift's "Tale of a Tub," based on his dissertation, was published in 1960 by Yale University Press.

Paulson held faculty positions in the English departments at the University of Illinois (1958-1963), Rice University (1963-1967), and Johns Hopkins University (1967-1975) where he was also department chair (1968-1975) and the Andrew W. Mellon Professor of the Humanities (1973-1975). In 1975 he returned to Yale to teach and to serve as director of Graduate Studies in the English Department (1976-1983), director of the British Studies Program (1976-1984), and a member of the Academic and Advisory Committees and Governing Board of the Yale Center for British Art and the Paul Mellon Centre for British Art in London (1975-1984). He also held the Thomas E. Donnelley Professorship from 1980 until 1984. In 1984 he went back to Johns Hopkins University where he was the William D. and Robin Mayer Professor of the Humanities; he was chair of the English Department from 1985 to 1991. Paulson retired from the university in 2005, and died at his home in Baltimore on August 7, 2024.

Two Festschriften were published during his lifetime: ELH Essays in Honor of Ronald Paulson , edited by Robert J. Griffin (Baltimore: Johns Hopkins University Press, 2005) and Representation, Heterodoxy, and Aesthetics: Essays in Honor of Ronald Paulson , edited by Ashley Marshall (Newark: University of Delaware Press, 2015). "Paulson's Progress," Marshall's introduction to the latter volume, is a fine and concise study of his life, work, and influence.

Ronald Paulson and Barbara Lee Appleton were married in New Haven in 1957 and divorced in 1983. The couple had a son, Andrew Meredith Paulson (1958-2017, Yale BA 1981), and a daughter, Melissa Katherine Paulson (1963-).

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Ronald Paulson Papers. The Lewis Walpole Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Ronald Paulson's papers were surveyed and packed by library staff at his home in Baltimore, Maryland. Much of the correspondence had been stored at his second home in Glen Rock, Pennsylvania, and was returned to Baltimore when that house was sold. Some of his papers suffered water damage in April 1970, and materials in Baltimore were affected by smoke damage by a fire in the next-door rowhouse in May 1998.

Fortunately, Paulson had housed his correspondence in nearly fifty repurposed ream boxes (from printed university letterhead and bond paper), so the boxes were affected rather than open files. Within the boxes, material was filed roughly in chronological order with dates generally inscribed on an end panel; some boxes were specific to year and month, some just a year. With Paulson's approval, library staff extracted most of the letters from the ream boxes and arranged them by correspondent to privilege his relationships with colleagues, friends, family, and the institutions he worked for or with (Boxes 1-26 and 30-36). At the end of that process, a small group of letters remained in place because they did not, in quantity of significance of content, earn a name-titled file; they were arranged chronologically and are in Boxes 27-29.

Additions to the archive after Paulson's death in 2024 included the thirteen family photo albums, his school class book for 1944, a ca. 1943 scrapbook of political cartoons, and ten diaries spanning 1947 to 1960. Also added to the collection were architectural drawings and sketches for a house and gardens, and a final group of loose prints, drawings, and cartoons.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Ronald Paulson Papers document the life and work of a leading scholar of British culture in the long eighteenth century. Paulson had a particular specialization in the artists, satirists, and social critics William Hogarth and Thomas Rowlandson, the authors Henry Fielding and Jonathan Swift, and the topics of satire and humor in general, but the papers reveal his interest in all aspects of literary criticism, visual culture, history, and art spanning the eighteenth through the twentieth centuries. The collection holds his professional and personal papers: diaries and daily journals, correspondence files, military service records, writings by and about him, and the research materials for his courses and publications.

Paulson's correspondence files comprise more than a third of the collection; they span his entire career and document his relationships with more than five hundred individuals and institutions in the United States and Great Britain, as well as with his family and the universities that employed him. He kept in touch with former colleagues after he changed schools, with students who became academic colleagues and those who left the field, and with personal friends from his childhood in Indiana and from his school years at Yale. His writing and research files form half of the collection and date from his earliest explorations of the themes he taught and published on for more than fifty years. In addition to documenting his life and career, Paulson's correspondence files are a resource for studying the ecosystem of university English departments and scholarly publishing in America during the second half of the twentieth century.

separatedmaterial.head: Separated Materials
separatedmaterial.p: A full set of Ronald Paulson's published books were received with his papers; they were removed to the book collection and cataloged with a call number beginning with "Paulson." Also cataloged separately are the prints by William Hogarth and other 18th-century printmakers that Paulson collected.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Ronald Paulson Papers is the physical property of the Lewis Walpole Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the W.S. Lewis Librarian/Executive Director.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series contains thousands of letters to and from Ronald Paulson's family, colleagues, and friends. The majority of the letters are filed in the first subseries, General Correspondence, with smaller groups of files for the four universities that employed him in the second subseries, Affiliated Universities Correspondence, and letters from his parents, children, and others in the third, Family Correspondence.

The earliest letters in the collection date from 1952, and are found in each subseries. Paulson maintained decades of written contact with Indiana school friends Paul Arnold and George Feldman, with Erik Woit from his Boy Scout days, and with Yale College friends including Jim Copeland and his freshman-year roommate Winthrop ("Windy") Beach. Also present in the files are other non-academics such as Texas poet Elizabeth Anne McBride (1942-2009), who befriended Paulson and his wife Barbara when they lived in Houston in the mid-1960s and babysat for their children; she enclosed poems in her letters for decade afterward.

The bulk of the correspondence, however, is with his professional colleagues: fellow academics at the universities he had taught at; former graduate students who became colleagues and coauthors; his editors and publishers at journals and university presses; organizations of which he was a member; and institutions with which he had long relationships, such as the Yale Center for British Art in New Haven and the Paul Mellon Centre for Studies in British Art in London. In one example, the Columbia University English professor James L. Clifford, thirty years Paulson's senior, wrote as a fan in 1960 and their correspondence continued until Clifford's death in 1978. A favorite student, Tom Mitchell, became a friend, colleague, and a forty-year correspondent. Other prominent eighteenth-century scholars and mentors populate the files, including Maynard Mack, J. Hillis Miller (who hired Paulson twice, first at Hopkins and then at Yale), Earl R. Wasserman, William K. Wimsatt, and the eminent Hopkins humanist Richard Macksey; the files hold letters from their wives as well. There is correspondence with colleagues asking for faculty search recommendations and departmental reviews at their schools, from university and academic presses seeking reader reports, from tenure committees looking for endorsements, and from universities offering propositions meant to lure him to their faculty posts.

In addition to teaching, Paulson served as an editor, senior editor, and editorial board member for several scholarly journals, most prominently ELH ( English Literary History , 1967–2005), Studies in English Literature (1963–2003), and Eighteenth-Century Studies (1966–1974), so there are numerous letters relating to those publications from authors, staff, and board members; in general, correspondence with authors is filed by author, and that with editors and staff is filed by the journal's title. He maintained advisor relationships with the National Endowment for the Humanities for over twenty years, and received fellowships from the John Simon Guggenheim Foundation, Rockefeller Foundation, Henry E. Huntington Library, and the American Philosophical Society. Paulson was a frequent and popular speaker and panelist at academic conferences, and presented series of lectures at the universities of Toronto, Puget Sound, Tennessee, Zurich, and Notre Dame University, in the 1970s and 80s. All of these organizations—and many more—are represented in Series I. As Paulson typed many of his outgoing letters, both personal and professional, much of his correspondence is two-way (with exceptions such as his use of aerograms for friends overseas).

Throughout the series there are letters related to Paulson's research and expertise on William Hogarth and other topics of keen interest, though this material does not aggregate in any particular spot except for one folder in Box 27 in the Chronological Files. Many people contacted Paulson over the years about Hogarth prints and paintings, some wanting his advice, some responding to his own queries. Not all were academics. Roy Clyde Gardner wrote several times from Union, Mississippi, hoping for authentication of a painting he owned that he was sure was by Hogarth, but Paulson did not support the attribution. He was also a resource for the British painter Ralph Lillford (1932-2019) who wrote to ask about the technicalities of Hogarth's engraved plates, and with Paulson's encouragement earned a PhD with a dissertation on that topic and a forty-five-year friendship treasured by both men.

The sections and their files in this series were assembled in 2020 from a sorting of Paulson's chronologically boxed letters by library staff, with his approval. Dates added in pencil in square brackets on a letter were assigned during processing based on surrounding letters; they were not confirmed by any other source. Letters that did not, in quantity or significance of content, earn a name-titled file remain in the Chronological Files in boxes 27 through 29.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series I is organized into three subseries: General Correspondence, Affiliated University Correspondence, and Family Correspondence.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized under two headings: Alphabetical Files, and Chronological Files.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The subseries holds files documenting Paulson's interactions with the four universities at which he studied and worked. The correspondence covers his employment and his official departmental activities but does not comprise English Department records at the schools. Correspondence with other university departments—Yale's libraries, museums, and university press, for instance—appears in the General Correspondence subseries preceding, as does his correspondence with colleagues at the universities. Folder 3 in Box 28 holds pages of largely personal notes documenting his struggle over whether to move to Yale (1972) and to go back to Johns Hopkins (1984).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged in chronological order of academic involvement.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Howard Clarence Paulson was born in Irving, Minnesota, on August 6, 1900, and graduated from the University of North Dakota in 1924 with a major in education. He was a public school teacher and superintendent in Carpio, Minot, Donnybrook, and Bottineau, North Dakota. His involvement with the Boy Scouts of America began as a volunteer scoutmaster in Minot in 1927 and in the mid-1930s he was working for the organization in Muncie, Indiana. In 1941 he moved to take a Scout position in Evansville, Indiana, and remained there until 1948 when he became assistant national director of professional training for the Boy Scouts, at the headquarters on the Schiff Scout Reservation in northern New Jersey. Paulson died there of brain cancer on March 21, 1957.

Ethel Florence Tvete Paulson was born in LaMoure, North Dakota, on December 28, 1901, the daughter of Louis O. and Elisabeth Johnson Tvete. She graduated from the University of North Dakota in 1924 with a major in English. Primarily a homemaker, she devoted her time to raising her son Ronald, but became a third-grade teacher after the death of her husband in 1957. Ethel Paulson died in Baltimore on August 16, 1987.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Barbara Lee Appleton was born in Nashville, Tennessee, the daughter of J. T. and Emily Appleton. She earned a BA degree from Vanderbilt University in 1955, an MA in English from Yale University in 1957, and an MS from the School of Library Service, Columbia University, in 1979. After holding jobs at Yale's Sterling Memorial Library and Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, where she worked on Langston Hughes's papers, Paulson continued her career in library special collections work at the Pierpont Morgan Library (1982-1986) and Columbia University Health Science Library's Rare Books Department before moving to the National Endowment for the Humanities' Preservation and Accession Division in 1990. She retired from the NEH in 2007.
The Paulsons were married on May 25, 1957, and divorced in 1983.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The correspondence between Ronald and Barbara Paulson exists in two sets. The first, in Box 34, primarily has letters he received from Barbara, but interfiled are carbon copies of his typed responses to her, some annotated by him with comments on what he had written. The second set, in Box 35, has the letters that Barbara Paulson received from Ronald Paulson. She returned them to him in 2022 so they could be kept with his papers. In many cases after their children were born, their letters were addressed both to each other and the children, and Barbara's letters often had addendum notes by the children.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Andrew Paulson was born on November 13, 1958, in New Haven, Connecticut. He was raised in Champaign, Illinois, Houston, and Baltimore, and received a BA in French literature and literary criticism from Yale University in 1981; he attended the Yale School of Drama but did not graduate. From 1982 to 1987 he lived in Berlin and Paris where he wrote fiction, and from 1988 to 1993 he worked as a fashion and advertising photographer in Paris and Milan. He moved to Moscow in 1993 and became involved in several entrepreneurial efforts there including work for the French publisher Hersant and the Bank of Moscow, as well as developing variety of publishing projects. In 2011 Paulson and his company Agon Limited were hired by the World Chess Federation to handle its media and marketing rights, and he gained a reputation for promoting chess tournaments. Paulson died in London on July 18, 2017, from complications due to lung cancer, and was survived by his parents, his sister, and his husband Loic Landry Tchouante.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Paulson had a close relationship with his son, particularly after Andy left the United States and began his entrepreneurial activities in Paris, London, and Moscow. In 1992 both men acquired fax machines and much of their correspondence was exchanged in that medium until they began to use email.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Melissa Paulson attended Cornell University and graduated from Beaver College (BS 1987) in Glenside, PA; she had a career as a physical therapist and is a fiber artist.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series holds material documenting Ronald Paulson's personal life, including records of his military service, his personal graphic collections and artwork, and files on legal issues and real estate. Also in the series are nearly fifty years of diaries and forty years of daily calendars that provide context to the correspondence in Series I.

Paulson began drawing in the 1940s and had considered a career as a cartoonist. He continued making sketches and figure and animal studies throughout his lifetime. The series holds a few folders of selected works that include illustration projects, as well as a series of printed World War II satirical cartoons that he collected. Also present in the Visual Materials subseries are three portrait photographs of Paulson taken by his son Andrew in 2005 in the alleyway behind Paulson's townhouse on St. Paul Street in Baltimore.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series II is organized into five subseries: Military Papers, Journals, Calendars, Visual Materials, and Other Papers.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Paulson served in the United States Army's First Cavalry Division from September 30, 1952, to September 15, 1954; he entered as a second lieutenant and was promoted to first lieutenant. Paulson prepared for overseas service at the Army's Field Artillery School at Fort Sill, Oklahoma, where he began keeping a journal on March 19, 1953. In the summer of 1953, he was stationed in Japan, at Chitose near Sapporo on the island of Hokkaido; the journey there took thirty days at sea, with stops at San Juan, Cartagena, Honolulu, and Sasebo. He received an Honorable Discharge from the Army on November 16, 1961.

For his journal, Paulson wrote on paper ranging from lined notebook pages to printed regiment letterhead, filling both sides of the sheets with manuscript or typed essays. The journal ran to just over a thousand pages before the last entry was made on August 16, 1954.

did.unittitle: U.S. Army draft cards and certificates

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: American military topographic maps of Eniwa and Hokkaido, Japan.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Paulson began making daily narrative notes while in high school. His journals include drafts of stories and the occasional drawing; in 1992 he began to type his notes and print them to file. The diaries in boxes 46a and 95-126 were acquired in 2024 after Paulson's death.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Entries cover the period from 18 September 1948 to 11 August 1949; the first page is dated "June, 1949," and Paulson notes that those entries from his Freshman year at Yale University were written down earlier and copied into this book at that later point.

odd.head: General
odd.p: Pages presumably for January through June 12 torn out.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This is the first of Paulson's early diaries written in Engineer's Field Transit Books. Much of the content of these early diaries focus on the health of his father, Howard Paulson, who passed away on March 22, 1957.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This is a pocket-size diary that overlaps with the larger format diaries he filled after January 1983.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dating is based on printouts used as note paper.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Paulson generally used commercial calendars published by museums to keep track of appointments and events.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged under five headings: Artwork by Paulson, World War II Political Satires, Portraits of Paulson, Other Prints, and Family Photo Albums.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Printed illustrations for a Boy Scouts of America scout badge program.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Four copies of a printed map of a Boy Scouts of America camp in New Harmony, Indiana; unclear if Paulson was involved in the design or illustration but they were kept in a folder with the designs he made for the scout's Wood Badge Course.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Paulson drew these for a school class while a senior in high school. They were based on an article he read in Life Magazine .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ink on paperboard caricature of French prime minister Pierre Laval by American political cartoonist Paule S. Loring for the Providence Journal-Bulletin .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ink on paperboard caricature of President Franklin D. Roosevelt by American political cartoonist Paule S. Loring for the Providence Journal-Bulletin .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fifty-eight printed political cartoons that appeared in a series of advertisements published in popular magazines during the war by the Philadelphia electronics company. They were drawn by leading American editorial cartoonists in support of the war effort "as an inspiration to the men and women who are helping to produce the weapons of victory." Printed reproductions of the illustrations (minus advertising copy) were distributed separately by the company.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Twenty-four printed political cartoons drawn by Harold M. Talburt that appeared during the war in Scripps-Howard newspapers. Printed reproductions of the illustrations, accompanied by a pamphlet containing information "...about Talburt," were distributed separately by the company in December 1943.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Crayon on paper political cartoon of the "United Nations African Victory," shown as a giant hand holding a saber inscribed "Mediterranean short cut to the Orient" over a cowering Japanese military man; by American cartoonist Charles Werner and inscribed to Ronald Paulson.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Crayon on paper caricature of Adolf Hitler, depicted as a skeleton pouring "soldiers 51 to 60 years old" from a stein onto a table; by American cartoonist Charles Werner.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Political cartoon by Vaughn Shoemaker, cartoonist at The Daily News, Chicago; inscribed to Ronald Paulson.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Political cartoon by Cecil Jensen, editorial cartoonist at the Chicago Daily News; inscribed to Ronald Paulson.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ink drawing cartoon strip by Chester Gould; inscribed to "R. Paulson" in upper right, with newspaper clipping "Copyright, 1943, by The Chicago Tribune" pasted near center.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ink and pencil cartoon drawing by Ding Darling.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Binding stamped "Photographs"; mostly photos of family and friends in North Dakota but with few captions.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Loose photos formerly laid in the album above (Box 105) mostly of the Paulson children but also photos of friends, homes, vacation spots.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Cover stamped "La Moure, ND". Family photo album, captioned with names and other commentary. Chiefly 1920s, with a few photos from the 1950s including photos of Paulson with his children in 1958 and 1963.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Loose photos from the above album (Box 106) mostly of Paulson as a child, Camp Redwood, family, and friends; with some postcards and clippings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Stamped design on brown leather-covered album, mostly with photos of Paulson as a child and young man, many with captions, and photos of family and friends from the 1930s-1940s; some later photos of Melissa and Andrew (1950s-1960s).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Album with black leather covers, the front stamped "Photographs." Family photos from Ron Paulson's childhood, including views of a Boy Scout camp and family road trips to national parks, Mt. Rushmore, Yellowstone, and others? With some formal portraits of his parents, possibly a wedding anniversary.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Spiral bound photo album with a bright green woven cloth cover with metal label glued to the front cover "Photo Album". Snapshots of Ron Paulson a teenager and young man, fishing, scouting, and at military training camp. No dates and few captions.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Gold woven cloth cover with metal label "Photo Album". A photo album with informal snapshots as well as formal shots of individuals and groups, several with the names of photographers or photoshops (and some dated 1948) stamped on the backs. With one calling card and a couple of small clippings. Penciled notes and stamps on the versos suggest that this album was assembled as a record of Paulson's work as editor and staff writer for his high school newspaper "The School Spirit" in Evansville, Indiana.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two red and black photo albums documenting Paulson's two years of service in the U.S. Army in the Pacific region, including shots of a transport ship's passage through the Panama Canal and sites in Hawaii and Japan, including Camp Hokkaido 82nd Field Artillery Battalion. Mounted on the final leaves of the second album are several snapshots and two professional shots of Paulson as a young academic in the 1960s and a series of professional portraits taken while serving as the Andrew W. Mellon Professor of Humanities at Johns Hopkins in 1974. Also an unidentified portrait of an academic colleague by Richard W. Linfield.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Green leather album with "Photographs" and decorative tooling in gold. A collection of postcards showing places in Norway, England, Scotland, Denmark, and Sweden where the Paulson family traveled in the summer of 1965.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A large brown leather binder decorated with gold tooling on the cover and spines. First page includes a shot of Ron Paulson in an office and two small head shots (passport style) of Ron and Barbara Paulson. The pages following include: a portrait of a young Frances Ferguson with a 1979 clipping from a Johns Hopkins publication laid in; many pages of pictures of Paulson in academic settings including one in which a Hogarth print is clearly laid out. The majority of the album is devoted to his close colleagues and friends, Susan de Sola and Joan Dayan with several pages of pictures of his mother the year of her death (1987); pictures of his trip to Italy and France with colleagues in 1987 and 1988; and many pictures of his pets in the Baltimore house and one picture of his son Andy.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photo album with plastic covers decorated with orange and yellow floral patterns; presumably assembled by Ronald Paulson's mother, mostly of shots of Ron and his parents as well as other family members and friends from the 1940s into the 1960s, and some dated as late as 1978; also some snapshots of her grandchildren, Andrew and Melissa.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Large brown leather binder with gold trim begins with a certificate in Russian as well as cartoons, a clipping, and photos from a trip to Leningrad and Moscow; a formal portrait of Andrew; views of the Paulsons homes in Hamden, CT; views of Glen Rock, PA; photos of professional and personal trips to Lisbon (1989), Norway (1989), and Jamaica (1992); photos of Melissa, her husband, and their two children, Matt and Sara (1995).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Large brown leather binder with gold trim begins with family photos of Christmas 1995 with the grandchildren and continues through 2005 with photos of family trips (Venice 2000) as well as professional trips with friends to Moscow, The Hague, and Jamaica; and many photos of pets. Printouts of photos and an email laid in at the back.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes undated telephone lists, an address book, a newspaper clipping, and Paulson's curriculum vitae from approximately 2018.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: During his extensive career, Ronald Paulson wrote and edited more than forty books and nearly two hundred essays and reviews of books, exhibitions and films; the topics were largely eighteenth-century literature, art, and culture, but he also published on contemporary art, cemeteries, and aspects of English and American culture. The series accounts for nearly all of those writings, as well as what others wrote about Paulson and his publications.

Primarily present here are tear sheets from academic journals and magazines, with some draft manuscripts and correspondence. The material is arranged to follow a bibliography Paulson gave with his papers. If an item is not present in the papers no box and folder number is given. Chapters in books are listed though the volumes were transferred to the library's book collection (a call number is given in the entry). Copies of the more than twenty books Paulson authored and gave with his papers were also removed to the book collection and can be located in the library's online catalog with a call number prefix of "Paulson"; except for two titles, the books are not listed in this finding guide.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series III is organized into two subseries: Writings about Paulson and his Works, and Writings by Paulson.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The folders here contain writings by Paulson from his high school and college years.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Paulson was on the staff of his student newspaper at Benjamin Bosse High School in Evansville, Indiana, where he wrote feature stories and columns.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Four homemade booklets holding Paulson's unsigned articles clipped and mounted; he annotated some of the articles and had passed the booklets to a teacher for criticism.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A group of manuscripts and typescripts that Paulson labeled as "...from prior to 1948 -- probably more like 1946. My earliest attempt at writing poetry."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Paulson served as contributing editor and editorial staff member in 1949, and as the journal's executive editor from February 1950 through March 1951; he also contributed essays and spot illustations. The magazine was published by the Chi Delta Theta Literary Society.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Paulson was an editorial associate of the journal from September 1949 through February 1952; he did not write for it but did contribute cartoon drawings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Three typed and undated short papers on the topics of Dryden, Anne Killigrew, and seventeenth- and twentieth-century criticism.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series holds material accumulated by Ronald Paulson in support of his research and writings. The folders contain a mix of his autograph and typed notes, photocopied journal articles and essays by colleagues, bibliographies, clippings from magazines and newspapers, and ephemera. Most of the material is undated. Some folders also include typed pages, draft sections, and fragments extracted from Paulson's writings, which he had filed for easier subject access; they are generally unsigned, undated, and often show autograph edits.

Folder titles were transcribed from Paulson's original files, with abbreviations spelled out and partial names listed in full. The files are generally in the order in which they arrived at the library; some rearranging was done to create an alphabetical order within each subseries. Order was not established within the folders, nor was material vetted for transfer to another folder, except in the case of correspondence. Some folders with extensive correspondence with publishers or colleagues were moved to Series I or letters were removed to be united with other letters from a person already in Series I.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series IV is organized into five subseries: Aesthetics, Art History and Criticism, Henry Fielding, William Hogarth, and Other Subjects and Topics.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The series contains publication-quality photographs, primarily black and white prints, collected by Paulson for his prolific research and publications on William Hogarth and other eighteenth-century artists. Photographs of satires and caricatures are most prevalent but also included are images of history paintings, genre works, landscapes, portraits, illustrations, and ephemera. Examples of earlier works are present for comparative purposes.

There are few annotations on the photographs indicating artist or title of the work, or date of the photograph, though many are stamped with museum/collection numbers or other identification data. Layout instructions for publication design are adhered to a large number of the photographs, though many have become detached and have separated from the relevant photograph.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series V is organized into three subseries: William Hogarth, Comparative Works and Sources, and Other Artists.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes photographs of Hogarth's home, a terracotta portrait bust by Louis François Roubiliac (National Portrait Gallery), his gravesite in St Nicholas's Churchyard, Chiswick, and other miscellaneous images.
